commonProblems.h2: Common Audi Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense

problems.group: 2.0 TFSI Oil Consumption & Piston Ring Wear
problems.h4: 2.0 TFSI Oil Consumption & Piston Ring Wear – Why Audi Petrol Engines Burn Oil Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Audi A3 8V, A4 B8/B9, A5 8T/8F, Q3 8U, Q5 8R (2008–2016, EA888 Gen 2/3 engine codes: CAEB, CABA, CCTA, CJEB)
problems.rootCause: Early EA888 engines used piston rings with insufficient tension and oil control, allowing excessive oil to enter the combustion chamber; combined with carbon buildup on intake valves, this accelerates wear and oil consumption.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace crankcase ventilation valve, update ECU software, clean intake valves via walnut blasting | May reduce oil burn temporarily; only viable if consumption is mild (<1L/1,000 miles) and piston rings are not yet worn |

| Remove cylinder head, replace piston rings and seals, clean carbon deposits, reassemble with updated components | Addresses root cause if caught before cylinder bore scoring; 20,000–40,000 mile extension if driving habits remain unchanged |

| Supply & fit of reconditioned EA888 with upgraded piston rings, revised PCV, updated timing components; bench-tested, 12‑month warranty | Restores factory performance with failure-point upgrades; ideal for high-mileage cars or where bore damage has occurred |

problems.recommendation: If your Audi is consuming under 1L per 1,500 miles and shows no blue smoke, start with the PCV update and walnut blast — it's cost-effective and non-invasive. However, if consumption exceeds 1L/500 miles or compression tests reveal bore wear, a rebuilt engine with upgraded rings offers better long-term value than repeated intermediate repairs. On an A4 or Q5 worth £4,000–£8,000, a £2,800–£4,200 rebuilt engine preserves the vehicle's value without main-dealer premiums.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt EA888 engine prices for your Audi A3/A4/Q5

problems.h4: 2.0 TDI DPF, EGR & Turbo Failures – The Urban-Driving Weakness in Audi Diesels
problems.affectedModels: Audi A3 8P/8V, A4 B8/B9, A6 C7, Q3 8U, Q5 8R (2008–2017, EA189 engine codes: CFFB, CFGB, CLJA, CRBC)
problems.rootCause: Short-trip urban driving prevents DPF regeneration cycles, causing soot accumulation that clogs the EGR cooler and increases exhaust backpressure; this strains the turbocharger and can trigger limp mode or catastrophic turbo failure.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Off-car DPF chemical clean, EGR valve removal/decarbon, forced regeneration via diagnostic tool | Effective only if DPF substrate is intact; requires regular motorway driving post-repair to maintain results |

| Replace DPF assembly, EGR cooler, turbo actuator; update software for improved regeneration logic | Addresses multiple failure points; suitable if engine block is healthy and mileage is under 100k |

| Supply & fit of reconditioned 2.0 TDI with reinforced EGR cooler, upgraded turbo bearings, revised DPF strategy; bench-tested, 12‑month warranty | Best value for high-mileage diesels or where repeated component failures indicate systemic wear; eliminates recurring repair costs |

problems.recommendation: For Audi diesels used primarily for short commutes, the minor DPF/EGR service is a sensible first step — but only if the vehicle will subsequently be driven on longer journeys to enable regeneration. If your A4 or Q5 has already entered limp mode multiple times, or if turbo whine is audible, investing in a rebuilt EA189 engine is more economical than replacing individual components that may fail again within 12–18 months.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt EA189 engines with uprated emissions components

problems.h4: 3.0 TDI Timing Chain Tensioner Failure – The Critical Weakness in Audi V6 Diesels
problems.affectedModels: Audi A6 C7, A7 4G, A8 D4, Q7 4L/4M (2010–2018, engine codes: CASA, CLAB, CDUC, CCWA, BUG)
problems.rootCause: The rear-mounted timing chain tensioner on the 3.0 TDI V6 can lose hydraulic pressure over time, allowing chain slack that leads to guide wear, timing drift, and in severe cases, piston-to-valve contact.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace hydraulic tensioner, chain guides, and seals; reset timing via diagnostic tool | Only viable if caught before chain stretch or guide fragmentation; requires engine removal on Q7/A8 models |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt 3.0 TDI V6 Engine |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned 3.0 TDI with revised tensioner design, upgraded guides, balanced rotating assembly; bench-tested, 12‑month warranty | The only realistic option once timing has drifted or valves are bent; restores reliability with failure-point upgrades for high-mileage SUVs and saloons |

problems.recommendation: Unlike many failures, the 3.0 TDI timing chain issue offers little warning — rattling may appear only hours before catastrophic failure. If your Q7 or A6 has exceeded 90,000 miles and has not had the tensioner updated, we strongly recommend either the preventative tensioner replacement (if budget allows) or planning for a rebuilt engine. Given the labour intensity of accessing the rear chain on Quattro models, a rebuilt engine with supply & fit often represents better value than a major engine-out repair.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 3.0 TDI engine prices for your Audi Q7/A6/A8

problems.h4: 1.4/1.8 TFSI Timing Chain Stretch & Carbon Buildup – Small Engine, Big Repair Bills
problems.affectedModels: Audi A1 8X, A3 8V, Q2 GA, TT 8J (2010–2018, engine codes: CAXA, CPTA, CXSA, CYVB)
problems.rootCause: The compact design of the 1.4/1.8 TFSI places the timing chain under high stress; combined with direct injection carbon buildup on intake valves, this accelerates chain stretch and tensioner wear, leading to misfires or no-start conditions.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Carbon Clean & Chain Tension Check |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Walnut-blast intake valves, inspect chain tension via diagnostic data, replace tensioner if stretch detected | Suitable only if chain stretch is minimal (<5°); does not address underlying design stress on high-mileage units |

repairOptions.tier: | Intermediate – Full Timing Chain Kit Replacement |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace timing chain, tensioner, guides, and sprockets; recalibrate variable valve timing | Addresses the immediate failure point; recommended if the engine is otherwise healthy and under 100k miles |

| Supply & fit of reconditioned unit with updated chain components, revised PCV, and carbon-mitigation strategy; bench-tested, 12‑month warranty | Best option for A1/A3/Q2 models where repeated timing issues indicate systemic wear; preserves vehicle value at a fraction of main-dealer replacement cost |

problems.recommendation: For Audi A1 or A3 owners under 80,000 miles, the intermediate timing chain kit replacement is a sound investment — but only if performed by a specialist familiar with TFSI calibration. If your vehicle has already suffered a no-start due to chain jump, or if compression tests reveal valve damage, a rebuilt engine is the only reliable path forward. Given the compact engine bay, supply & fit from a specialist often completes the job faster and cheaper than a main dealer.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt 1.4/1.8 TFSI engines for your Audi A1/A3/Q2

problems.group: EA888 High-Pressure Fuel Pump & Cam Follower Wear
problems.h4: EA888 High-Pressure Fuel Pump & Cam Follower Wear – The Hidden Cost of Direct Injection
problems.affectedModels: Audi A4 B8/B9, A5 8T/8F, Q5 8R, TT 8J (2008–2016, EA888 Gen 2 engine codes: CAEB, CCTA, CABA)
problems.rootCause: The high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) is driven by a cam lobe on the intake camshaft; inadequate lubrication or hardened cam followers cause rapid wear, leading to metal debris circulating in the oil and potential camshaft scoring.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace HPFP, cam follower, and oil filter; flush oil system to remove metal particles | Only effective if caught before camshaft scoring; requires regular oil changes with correct spec thereafter |

| Remove cylinder head cover, replace scored camshaft(s) and followers, clean oil galleries, update HPFP drive | Addresses damage after follower failure; suitable if block and pistons are undamaged and mileage is moderate |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt EA888 with HPFP Upgrade |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned EA888 with revised HPFP drive, upgraded cam followers, and improved oiling; bench-tested, 12‑month warranty | Recommended when metal debris has circulated through the block or when repeated camshaft failures indicate systemic oiling issues |

problems.recommendation: EA888 owners should inspect the cam follower at every major service (every 20k miles). If your Audi shows low fuel pressure codes or metallic noise from the valve cover, act immediately — delaying can turn a £400 preventative fix into a £2,700+ rebuild. For vehicles over 90k miles or with known oil consumption, a rebuilt engine with the HPFP upgrade offers peace of mind and avoids recurring camshaft repairs.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt EA888 engine prices with HPFP upgrade for your Audi A4/A5/Q5

engineSizes.h2: Audi Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model

items.title: Audi 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: A compact, high-efficiency turbo-diesel designed primarily for entry-level Audi hatchbacks and compact saloons, this displacement balances low running costs with respectable urban torque. Introduced in the late 2000s, it relies on a common-rail direct injection architecture that prioritises fuel economy but places high thermal stress on emissions and timing components. When replacement becomes necessary, it typically follows unresolved regeneration cycles or mechanical timing wear that compromises long-term viability. Engine code(s): CAYC, CXXB, CXXD, DBKA, CRKA Compatible models (UK): A1, A3, A4, Q3 Production years: 2009–present Common failure points: DPF blockage from short-trip driving, timing chain tensioner wear at higher mileages
items.cta: Get quotes for Audi 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Audi 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The most widely deployed diesel displacement in the Audi range, this 2.0-litre unit powers everything from compact executive saloons to mid-size SUVs across multiple generations. Renowned for strong low-end torque and refined highway cruising, it has evolved through EA189 and EA288 platforms to meet tightening emissions standards. Replacement demand spikes when repeated DPF/EGR system failures overwhelm the engine block or when high-pressure oil pump drives fracture internally. Engine code(s): BKD, BMM, CAGA, CFFB, CFGB, CRBC, CZPA, DTSA Compatible models (UK): A1, A3, A4, A5, A6, Q3, Q5 Production years: 2004–present Common failure points: EGR cooler cracking, turbo actuator seizure, oil pump drive splines wear
items.cta: Get quotes for Audi 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Audi 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: A precision-engineered V6 displacement built for Audi’s premium saloons, estates, and larger SUVs where refined power delivery and towing capability are essential. Featuring an aluminium block and intercooled twin-scroll turbocharging, this unit delivers smooth performance but places complex oiling and timing demands on the drivetrain. Replacement is most commonly triggered by catastrophic timing chain tensioner failure at the rear of the block or persistent oil cooler leaks that compromise bearing lubrication. Engine code(s): BKN, ASB, CASA, CLAB, CDUC, CVMD, DCPC Compatible models (UK): A4, A6, A7, A8, Q5, Q7 Production years: 2004–present Common failure points: Rear-mounted timing chain tensioner failure, oil cooler seal degradation, turbo boost pipe cracking
items.cta: Get quotes for Audi 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Audi 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: Developed as a lightweight, fuel-efficient petrol unit for Audi’s compact and subcompact line-up, this displacement bridges the gap between basic commuting engines and performance-oriented turbocharged platforms. Utilising TFSI direct injection, it delivers responsive power while keeping emissions low, though early iterations experienced notable wear in high-stress valvetrain components. Replacement becomes necessary when timing chain stretch or carbon-induced valve seating issues compromise compression beyond economical repair. Engine code(s): CAXA, CPTA, CZDA, CZEA, CZTA, DUNA Compatible models (UK): A1, A3, A4, Q2, Q3 Production years: 2008–present Common failure points: Timing chain stretch and guide fragmentation, HPFP cam follower wear, intake valve carbon buildup
items.cta: Compare Audi 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Audi 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: Positioned as a mid-range performance and touring unit, the 1.8-litre TFSI bridges the gap between compact daily drivers and executive saloons across the Audi portfolio. Featuring a robust cast-iron or early aluminium block with direct injection and turbocharging, it offers strong mid-range torque but has historically struggled with oil management and valvetrain timing under high thermal load. Replacement is typically pursued after repeated timing chain tensioner replacements or severe piston ring wear causes unsustainable oil consumption. Engine code(s): BZB, CDAA, CDHA, CJEB, CYFB Compatible models (UK): A3, A4, A5, Q5, TT Production years: 2007–2018 Common failure points: Timing chain tensioner hydraulic failure, PCV valve diaphragm rupture, cylinder bore scoring from oil starvation
items.cta: Compare Audi 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Audi 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The cornerstone of Audi’s petrol lineup, this 2.0-litre TFSI/TSI displacement powers everything from front-wheel-drive hatchbacks to quattro-equipped sports cars and SUVs. Evolving through EA888 generations, it delivers high specific output and excellent efficiency, but early Gen 2 and Gen 3 units are heavily documented for piston ring design flaws that lead to excessive oil consumption. When oil burn exceeds safe limits or internal carbonisation triggers misfires, a precision-rebuilt replacement unit offers the most reliable long-term solution. Engine code(s): AXX, BPY, BWA, CAEB, CCZB, CCTA, CJXC, CJXE, DLBA Compatible models (UK): A3, A4, A5, A6, Q3, Q5, TT, RS3, S3 Production years: 2004–present Common failure points: Piston ring tension failure (Gen 2/3), HPFP cam lobe wear, carbon-induced intake valve leakage, coolant leak from water pump housing
items.cta: Compare Audi 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Audi 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Engineered for Audi’s performance saloons, coupes, and flagship SUVs, the 3.0-litre V6 petrol engine combines supercharging or turbocharging with refined balance shafts to deliver seamless power delivery. Built on the EA839 platform, it prioritises high-revving responsiveness and torque consistency, but the complex timing chain routing and supercharger drive couplers introduce maintenance-heavy wear points over high mileage. Replacement is generally recommended once timing guide degradation or internal supercharger wear causes irreversible performance loss. Engine code(s): BBA, CTWA, CREC, DLBA, DPW, DPKW Compatible models (UK): A4, A5, A6, A7, A8, Q5, Q7, S4, S5, RS4, RS5 Production years: 2008–2023 Common failure points: Timing chain guide wear (front/rear), supercharger coupler degradation, coolant intrusion from valley plate seals
items.cta: Compare Audi 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Audi 4.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Reserved for Audi’s flagship performance and luxury models, the 4.0-litre V8 TFSI represents the pinnacle of brand-wide petrol displacement engineering, utilising twin-scroll turbocharging and active cylinder management. Designed for explosive acceleration and refined highway cruising, it operates under extreme thermal and mechanical loads that accelerate oil consumption and coolant system degradation in high-performance applications. When internal scoring or turbo oil feed failures occur, a factory-spec rebuilt unit restores original performance parameters with upgraded sealing components. Engine code(s): CTUA, CTUB, CWGD, DKRB, DNB Compatible models (UK): A6, A7, A8, Q7, Q8, RS6, RS7, S6, S7, e-tron GT Production years: 2012–present Common failure points: High-rev oil consumption from piston ring clearance, turbo oil feed line degradation, valley cover coolant leaks
items.cta: Compare Audi 4.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Audi 2.0L Hybrid Engines
items.description: Combining a turbocharged petrol displacement with integrated electric motor assistance, these plug-in hybrid powertrains deliver zero-emission urban mobility and extended range for longer journeys. Primarily deployed in Audi’s executive saloons and mid-size SUVs, the system relies on precise thermal management and high-voltage inverter coordination to optimise battery charging and regenerative braking. Replacement demand is highly specialist and typically relates to inverter module faults or long-term battery cell degradation rather than mechanical engine failure. System / motor code(s): DLTA, DKFA, TFSI e series, e-motor 135kW/140kW Compatible models (UK): A6, A7, A8, Q5, Q7 Production years: 2019–present Important notes: High-voltage battery health and inverter cooling integrity are critical; mechanical failure rare, specialist diagnostics required
items.cta: Enquire about Audi 2.0L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Audi Electric Motors
items.description: Audi’s fully electric range replaces traditional combustion displacements with high-output synchronous and asynchronous electric motors paired with liquid-cooled battery architectures. These powertrains feature instant torque delivery, regenerative braking integration, and advanced thermal management, making mechanical wear virtually negligible compared to internal combustion units. Replacement is exceptionally rare and generally isolated to inverter failure, stator winding degradation, or coolant ingress in the rear drive unit. System / motor code(s): APP 275, APP 310, e-tron 40/50/55, RS e-tron GT motors Compatible models (UK): e-tron 50/55, Q4 e-tron, Q8 e-tron, RS e-tron GT Production years: 2018–present Important notes: Motor failure rare; battery pack and power electronics degradation drive specialist replacement needs Not sure which engine size your Audi needs? Enter your registration number and we'll match you to the exact engine code and displacement — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Audi specialists.

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Audi Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Audi engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Audi specialists.

entries.title: CAGA 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to the A4 B8, A5 8T and Q5 8R from 2008–2015, this Common Rail TDI is a staple of UK Audi ownership. High-mileage units frequently fail due to seized EGR valves, DPF restriction causing turbo over-speed, and injector seal degradation leading to oil contamination. Our UK-rebuilt replacements include upgraded injector copper seals, cleaned or bypassed EGR systems, and balanced turbocharger assemblies.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CAGA Audi engine replacement

entries.title: BKD 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Used across the A3 8P, A4 B7 and TT 8J (2005–2009), this early-generation unit is notorious for balance shaft drive gear stripping and sudden crankshaft walk. Secondary issues include high oil consumption from coked piston rings. Replaced units feature a solid crankshaft-to-oil-pump drive coupling, revised low-friction piston rings, and full bottom-end clearance checks.

entries.title: BXE 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Found in the A3 8P, A4 B7 and Golf 5 (2003–2008), this code shares the PD architecture with BKD but suffers catastrophic oil pump spline shearing. The failure causes immediate bearing starvation and crankshaft seizure. Specialist rebuilds install a machined solid oil pump drive, reinforced big-end bearings, and a new high-flow oil pressure relief valve.

entries.title: CBAB 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Powers the A4 B8, A5 8T and Q5 8R (2008–2014) with improved CRDI efficiency. Owners typically seek replacement when HPFP cam follower wear causes metal contamination in the oil gallery, or turbo actuator motors stick. Upgraded replacements include hardened HPFP roller followers, reinforced turbo wastegate linkages, and complete oil gallery flushing protocols.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CBAB Audi replacement engines

entries.title: CRBC 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Installed in the A3 8V, A4 B9 and Q3 (2013–2019) as part of the cleaner EA288 generation. Common failure triggers are carbon buildup on intake valves, timing chain tensioner spring fatigue, and DPF regeneration faults. Quality rebuilt units receive revised tensioner pistons with stronger return springs, walnut-blasted intake ports, and updated oil catch cans.

entries.title: DFGA 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to post-2019 A3 8Y, A4 B9 and Q5 FY models to meet WLTP emissions standards. Failure typically stems from AdBlue crystallisation blocking SCR injectors and internal EGR cooler leaks contaminating coolant. Reconditioned units include reinforced SCR injector housings, upgraded EGR cooler cores with anti-leak seals, and revised coolant thermostats.

entries.title: CCTA 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Widely used in the A3 8P, A4 B8 and TT 8J (2008–2012), this first-generation direct-injection petrol engine is defined by severe oil consumption. The primary failure stems from early piston ring design allowing oil past into the combustion chamber, alongside timing chain guide wear. Rebuilt engines feature revised multi-stage piston rings, upgraded tensioner rails, and replaced PCV breather membranes.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CCTA Audi TFSI engines

entries.title: CAEB 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Powering the A4 B8, A5 8T and Q5 8R (2009–2015), this higher-output variant suffers from PCV diaphragm rupture causing massive vacuum leaks and excessive oil burning. Water pump impeller separation is also common. Specialist rebuilds include an OEM-spec PCV valve assembly, revised metal water pump impellers, and updated cam adjuster phasers.

entries.title: CCZA 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to the A3 8P/8V, Q3 and Golf 6 (2009–2017). Failure is predominantly driven by timing chain rattle due to hydraulic tensioner pressure loss and carbon-induced misfires. Replaced units receive Gen3-style hydraulic tensioners, reinforced nylon chain guides, and updated intake flap mechanisms for reliable operation.

entries.title: CDAA 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Used across the A4 B8, A5 8T and Q5 8R (2008–2014). Auxiliary belt tensioner bearing collapse and turbo oil feed line coking are the main catastrophic failure points leading to bearing starvation. Quality rebuilds feature high-flow turbo oil feed lines, reinforced tensioner pulleys, and revised crank pulley dampers to prevent recurrence.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CDAA Audi replacement engines

entries.title: CYFB 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Installed in the A3 8V, A4 B9 and Q5 (2014–2020) as part of the Gen3 evolution. High-pressure fuel pump internal wear and plastic thermostat housing cracks causing coolant loss drive most replacement requests. Reconditioned engines include revised HPFP plunger kits, machined thermostat housings with metal reinforcement, and updated oil separators.

entries.title: DKZA 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to the A4 B9, A5 F5 and Q5 FY (2018–2025) with updated emissions hardware. Failure is often linked to GPF backpressure damaging turbo bearings and oil dilution from frequent short-trip driving. Specialist replacements use heavy-duty turbo bearing journals, revised GPF differential pressure sensors, and low-dilution piston crowns.

entries.title: CASA 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Powers the A8 D3/D4, Q7 4L/4M and A6 C6 (2007–2016) with twin-turbo V6 efficiency. Swirl flap linkage snapping and turbo vane actuator sticking are the most common catastrophic failures on high-mileage examples. UK-rebuilt units include delete or reinforced swirl flap mechanisms, upgraded turbo actuator motors, and renewed injector return lines.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CASA Audi V6 engines

entries.title: BUG 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Found in the Q7 4L and A8 D3 (2006–2010), this early V6 suffers from oil cooler gasket failure leading to coolant-oil mixing, and crankshaft pulley damper separation. Replacements feature multi-layer steel oil cooler gaskets, revised harmonic balancers with improved bonding, and new injector copper seals to ensure leak-free operation.

entries.title: CCWA 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Installed in the A4 B8, A5 8T and Q5 8R (2008–2015). Failure typically centres on rear cam cover chain tensioner wear, causing chain stretch and valve timing drift. Specialist rebuilds install rear-mounted updated tensioner assemblies, reinforced cam phaser sprockets, and a fully balanced timing chain set to eliminate rattle.

entries.title: CDUD 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Powers the A6 C7, A7 4G and Q7 4M (2011–2018) with high torque output. Water pump plastic impeller fracture causing rapid overheating and EGR valve carbon seizure drive most replacements. Reconditioned units receive CNC-machined aluminium water pumps, ultrasonically cleaned EGR housings with new valves, and reinforced coolant hoses.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CDUD Audi replacement engines

entries.title: CDUC 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to the A6 C7, A7 4G and Q7 4M (2011–2019) as a higher-output variant. Turbo oil drain pipe coking and high-pressure fuel injector tip erosion are the primary failure points. Quality rebuilt units include stainless steel turbo drain pipes, newly calibrated piezo injectors, and revised high-pressure oil galleries for sustained performance.

entries.title: CGWB 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: (Supercharged V6) Powers the A6 C7, A7 4G and Q7 4M (2011–2018) with strong mid-range pull. Eaton supercharger intercooler seal leaks and timing chain guide wear on high-mileage examples are the primary replacement drivers. Specialist rebuilds feature supercharged-specific intercooler gaskets, reinforced chain guides, and upgraded oil pump pressure valves.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CGWB Audi supercharged engines

entries.title: CRED 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: (Supercharged V6) Fitted to the A6 C7, A7 4G and Q7 4M (2014–2019) with refined forced induction mapping. Failure stems from supercharger coupler degradation causing power loss and PCV failure leading to seal blowouts. Rebuilt units receive reinforced supercharger drive couplers, revised crankcase breather assemblies, and upgraded valve stem seals.

entries.title: CREC 2.5L Petrol
entries.description: (5-Cylinder Turbo) The iconic RS3 8V and TT RS FV powerplant (2015–2022) delivering extreme performance. Water pump/thermostat housing plastic failure causing catastrophic overheating and turbo oil feed restriction are the main catastrophic triggers. Replacements include billet aluminium thermostat housings, high-flow turbo oil feed lines, and reinforced cylinder head gaskets.

entries.title: CTEA 4.0L Petrol
entries.description: (V8 Twin-Turbo) Used in the A6 C7, S8 D4 and RS6/RS7 (2012–2018) for flagship performance. Cylinder bank imbalance causing premature rod bearing wear and active engine mount hydraulic failure drive replacement. Quality rebuilds feature precision-balanced rotating assemblies, reinforced main/rod bearings, and updated engine mount brackets.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CTEA Audi V8 engines

entries.title: CULF 1.8L Petrol
entries.description: Installed in the A3 8V, A4 B9 and Q3 (2012–2019) as an efficiency-focused TFSI variant. Timing chain stretch under high load and direct injection carbon deposits on intake ports are the dominant failure points. Reconditioned units receive heavy-duty timing chain sets, updated intake port cleaning protocols, and revised oil separator baffles. Closing line (Block A): Can’t find your engine code listed above? engineYears.h2: Audi Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When

years.description: Short intro: The late 1990s saw Audi carry forward its iconic five-cylinder units alongside the first wave of modern TDI diesels that would come to define the brand in the UK. Key Changes 1.8T (AEB, AGU, ARK) turbocharged petrol introduced across A3, A4, and A6 - a landmark unit for performance tuning First-generation TDI diesels with distributor pump technology (AFN, AHH, AHU, AVF) fitted across A4 and A6 2.5 V6 TDI (AKN) introduced on A6 and A8 for executive segment 2.8 V6 petrol (ACK, ALG) carried across A4, A6, and A8 as refined upper-trim option Early quattro drivetrains paired with naturally aspirated and turbocharged units Main Engines AEB / AGU / ARK - 1.8T petrol (Audi A3, A4, A6) AFN / AHU / AVF - 1.9 TDI diesel with distributor pump (Audi A4, A6) AKN - 2.5 V6 TDI diesel (Audi A6, A8) ACK / ALG - 2.8 V6 petrol (Audi A4, A6, A8) AJQ / AMU / APX - 1.8T petrol (Audi TT) Popular Models Audi A4 (B5) Audi A6 (C5) Audi A3 (8L) Audi TT (8N) Audi A8 (4D) Known For 1.8T AEB is one of the most tuneable petrols Audi ever produced, with a strong aftermarket following to this day Distributor pump TDIs (AFN, AHU) are regarded as bulletproof by diesel specialists - simpler injection mechanics means high-mileage survival is common Oil sludge build-up on 1.8T units at higher mileage if service intervals were stretched - well-documented in the enthusiast community Cam follower wear on 1.8T direct injection pump cars is a known failure point requiring early inspection [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: AEB, AGU, ARK, AJQ, AMU, APP, APX, AFN, AHH, AHU, AVF, AKN, ACK, ALG, ANB, APU
years.cta: Compare prices on 1997–2000 Audi replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: Common Rail TDI Arrives & PD Diesel Expansion 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Audi's adoption of pump-duse (PD) direct injection diesel technology transformed fuel economy and refinement across the A3, A4, A6, and A8 - and became the most widely replaced diesel family of the following decade. Key Changes Pump Duse (PD) unit injector diesels replace distributor pump units across the range - higher injection pressures and improved efficiency 1.9 TDI PD (AVB, BKE, AXR) introduced on A3 and A4 at 100 and 130ps outputs 2.0 TDI PD (BLB, BKD, BMM) introduced in A4 (B6) in 2003 - a foundational unit across multiple models for a decade 3.0 V6 TDI (ASB, BMK) introduced on A6 and A8 for premium segment diesels 4.2 V8 (BAS, BAT, BBJ) petrol continues on S4 and RS6 - defining era of naturally aspirated V8 performance Main Engines AVB / BKE / AXR - 1.9 TDI PD diesel (Audi A3, A4) BKD / BLB / BMM - 2.0 TDI PD diesel (Audi A4, A6) ASB / BMK - 3.0 V6 TDI diesel (Audi A6, A8) ARE / BES - 4.2 V8 petrol (Audi S4, RS6) AWM / AMB - 1.8T petrol evolution (Audi A4, A6) Popular Models Audi A4 (B6) Audi A6 (C5 late / C6 early) Audi A3 (8P early) Audi S4 (B6) Audi RS6 (C5) Audi A8 (4E) Known For BKD 2.0 TDI PD is one of the most common engine replacement searches in UK - EGR valve failures and DPF problems at 80,000–120,000 miles are well-documented PD units run at extremely high injection pressures - injector seal leaks cause oil contamination of coolant and are a priority inspection point at service 4.2 V8 RS6 twin-turbo engine is beloved by enthusiasts but timing chain tensioner failures are catastrophic and expensive when neglected Strong residual demand for reconditioned 2.0 TDI PD units from A4 (B6/B7) owners - one of the highest UK replacement volumes of any Audi engine [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: AVB, BKE, AXR, BKD, BLB, BMM, ASB, BMK, ARE, BES, AWM, AMB, AVF, BPP, AUK
years.cta: Find reconditioned 2001–2004 Audi TDI and V8 engines with UK warranty

years.preview: FSI Direct Injection Petrol & Third-Generation A6 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Audi's rollout of FSI (Fuel Stratified Injection) direct-injection petrol technology across the A3, A4, A6, and TT signalled the shift away from indirect injection - introducing efficiency gains alongside new carbon build-up challenges. Key Changes 2.0 FSI and TFSI (AXW, BLR, BLX, BLY, BWE) introduced across A3, A4, and TT - first wave of direct injection petrols Fourth-generation A6 (C6) launched with 2.4 V6, 3.2 FSI V6, and 2.7/3.0 TDI engines 3.2 FSI V6 (AUK, BKH) introduced on A3, A4, and TT RS - large-capacity naturally aspirated option 2.7 TDI (BAU, BPP, CANA) replaces 2.5 V6 TDI on A6 and A8 Euro 4 compliance across the range - DPF fitment begins across diesel models Main Engines AXW / BLR / BLX / BWE - 2.0 FSI petrol (Audi A3, A4) BWA / BPY - 2.0 TFSI petrol turbo (Audi A3, A4, TT) AUK / BKH - 3.2 V6 FSI petrol (Audi A3, A6, TT) BAU / BPP / CANA - 2.7 TDI diesel (Audi A6, A8) BRD / BDG - 3.0 TDI V6 diesel (Audi A6, Q7) Popular Models Audi A3 (8P) Audi A4 (B7) Audi A6 (C6) Audi TT (8J) Audi Q7 (4L) Known For 2.0 TFSI (BWA, BPY) on A3 and A4 B7 noted for carbon deposit build-up on intake valves - a characteristic of direct injection requiring periodic walnut blasting 2.7 TDI on A6 has strong torque delivery but turbocharger actuator failures are common at 100,000+ miles Q7 3.0 TDI (BUG, BKN) introduced significant SUV diesel demand and associated DPF regeneration issues on short-journey use First-generation DPF fitment across diesels generates ongoing filter replacement and regen issues - highest failure rate in cold, urban UK conditions [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: AXW, BLR, BLX, BLY, BWE, BWA, BPY, AUK, BKH, BAU, BPP, CANA, BRD, BDG, BUG, BKN, AHC
years.cta: Browse 2005–2007 Audi FSI and TDI replacement engine quotes

years.preview: EA888 Petrol Launch & New TFSI Generation 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The introduction of the EA888 turbocharged 2.0 TFSI family in 2008 marked a generation-defining shift in Audi's petrol architecture - replacing the EA113 and becoming the core unit across A4, A5, Q5, and TT for the next decade. Key Changes EA888 Gen 1 2.0 TFSI (CDNB, CDNC, CAEB, CAEA) launches across A4 (B8), A5, and Q5 - replaces EA113 family 1.8 TFSI (BYT, CDAA, CJSA) introduced on A3, A4, and TT as the new entry-level turbo petrol 2.0 TDI common rail (CAGA, CAGB, CAHB) replaces PD diesels on A4 B8 and A5 - cleaner Euro 4/5 transition V6 petrol S and RS models see engine codes CDN, CESC across S4, S5, and S6 4.2 V8 FSI (BNS, BVJ) continues on R8 and S5 Coupé Main Engines CAEB / CDNB / CDNC - 2.0 TFSI petrol EA888 Gen 1 (Audi A4, A5, Q5) BYT / CDAA / CJSA - 1.8 TFSI petrol (Audi A3, A4, TT) CAGA / CAGB / CAHB - 2.0 TDI common rail diesel (Audi A4, A5, Q5) CDN / CESC - 3.0 V6 TFSI petrol supercharged (Audi S4, S5, S6) BNS / BVJ - 4.2 V8 FSI (Audi R8, S5) Popular Models Audi A4 (B8) Audi A5 (8T) Audi Q5 (8R) Audi A3 (8P facelift) Audi TT (8J) Audi S4 (B8) Known For EA888 Gen 1 is known for timing chain stretch at low mileage on the CAEB unit - tensioner failures reported between 60,000–100,000 miles on early cars, a major driver of replacement demand S4 / S5 3.0 V6 TFSI supercharged is praised for silky linear power delivery and durability, but supercharger snout seal failures add maintenance cost 2.0 TDI common rail marks a significant reliability improvement over PD predecessors - DPF issues remain but injector reliability is meaningfully better 4.2 V8 in R8 and S5 is a high-revving naturally aspirated highlight - robust but high rebuild costs when oil consumption issues develop [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: CAEB, CDNB, CDNC, CAEA, CDAA, BYT, CJSA, CAGA, CAGB, CAHB, CGLB, CGLA, CDN, CESC, BNS, BVJ, CDUC
years.cta: Get quotes for 2008–2010 Audi EA888 and TDI replacement engines

years.preview: EA888 Gen 2 & Euro 5 Consolidation 🔴 Major Reliability Revision
years.description: Short intro: The EA888 second generation brought key internal revisions - including improved timing chain guidance and revised cooling - making 2011-onward units measurably more robust than their Gen 1 predecessors, while Euro 5 compliance was standardised across all diesel models. Key Changes EA888 Gen 2 (CNHA, CJCA, CPKA) introduced - timing chain tensioner revised, updated oil circuit to address Gen 1 chain stretch New 1.4 TFSI (CAXA, CAXC) twincharged and single-turbo variants roll out across A1, A3, and Sportback 2.0 TDI (CGLB, CGLD, CLJA) updated for Euro 5 with improved EGR management across A4, A5, A6 3.0 TDI V6 (CGQB, CKVB) revised for improved DPF regeneration and SCR preparation A1 (8X) launched with 1.2 TFSI (CBZA), 1.4 TFSI, and 1.6 TDI - broadening Audi's engine range into the supermini segment Main Engines CNHA / CJCA / CPKA - 2.0 TFSI EA888 Gen 2 petrol (Audi A4, A5, Q5, TT) CAXA / CAXC - 1.4 TFSI petrol (Audi A1, A3) CBZA / CBZB - 1.2 TFSI petrol (Audi A1, A3) CGLB / CGLD - 2.0 TDI Euro 5 diesel (Audi A4, A5, A6) CGQB / CKVB - 3.0 TDI V6 diesel (Audi A6, A7, Q7) CESA / CDMA - 2.0 TFSI petrol (Audi TT S) Popular Models Audi A4 (B8 facelift) Audi A6 (C7) Audi A5 (8T facelift) Audi A1 (8X) Audi Q5 (8R) Audi A7 (4G) Known For Gen 2 EA888 units are regarded by UK engine specialists as considerably more reliable than Gen 1 - chain-stretch replacements drop sharply from 2011 onwards 1.4 TFSI twincharged (CAXA) is admired for refinement and economy, though actuator failures on the compressor bypass valve are a recurring servicing point 3.0 TDI on A6/A7 earns strong owner praise for long-distance motorway performance, but high-pressure fuel pump failures and injector issues at 130,000+ miles are widely reported A1 1.2 TFSI generates the largest supermini replacement enquiry volume among Audi models in the UK urban driver market [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: CNHA, CJCA, CPKA, CAXA, CAXC, CBZA, CBZB, CGLB, CGLD, CLJA, CGQB, CKVB, CESA, CDMA, CREA
years.cta: Find 2011–2013 Audi reconditioned engine prices from UK suppliers

years.preview: EA888 Gen 3, EA189 Dieselgate & Euro 6 Transition 🔴 Major Reliability Revision 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: These years brought Audi's most significant diesel crisis - the EA189 Dieselgate recall affecting hundreds of thousands of UK cars - while simultaneously introducing the more refined EA288 diesel and the third-generation EA888 petrol, raising the bar for emissions and reliability. Key Changes EA888 Gen 3 (CHHA, CHHB, CJSA, DJHA) launched across A3, A4, A5 - revised cylinder head, improved thermal management, and updated chain guide EA189 diesel scandal breaks in September 2015 - UK recall of affected 1.6 and 2.0 TDI models (CAYC, CFHC, CLIA, CLCB codes) EA288 2.0 TDI (CRBC, CRLB, DFCA) begins replacing EA189 units across A3 and A4 - Euro 6 ready 1.4 TFSI cylinder deactivation (COD) system introduced on A3 and A4 - fuel economy focus Audi A3 e-tron PHEV launched in UK - 1.4 TFSI petrol paired with electric motor Main Engines CHHA / CHHB / DJHA - 2.0 TFSI EA888 Gen 3 petrol (Audi A3, A4, A5, Q5) CRBC / CRLB / DFCA - 2.0 TDI EA288 diesel Euro 6 (Audi A3, A4) CZCA / CZDA - 1.4 TFSI petrol with COD (Audi A3, A4) CNWD - 1.6 TDI diesel (Audi A3, A4) CJXA - 1.4 TFSI e PHEV petrol (Audi A3 e-tron) Popular Models Audi A3 (8V) Audi A4 (B8 late / B9 early) Audi A5 (8T facelift) Audi Q3 (8U) Audi Q5 (8R facelift) Audi A3 e-tron Known For EA189 recall creates lasting depreciation and owner uncertainty - cars receiving software-only fix maintain soot accumulation concerns among independent specialists EA888 Gen 3 is broadly considered Audi's most refined modern petrol - carbon build-up on intake valves remains but is reduced; chain durability is markedly improved EA288 diesel is a genuine Euro 6 engineering improvement over EA189 - fewer injector seal issues, though DPF and AdBlue system maintenance costs increase 1.4 TFSI with COD cylinder deactivation praised for fuel economy but actuator sealing and oil consumption on high-mileage units require monitoring [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: CHHA, CHHB, DJHA, CRBC, CRLB, DFCA, CZCA, CZDA, CNWD, CAYC, CFHC, CLIA, CLCB, CJXA, DKRA
years.cta: Compare 2014–2016 Audi engine replacements - EA288, EA888 Gen 3, and EA189 recall units

years.preview: A4 B9 Full Range, 48V MHEV Debut & PHEV Expansion 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Audi's 2017–2019 period introduced 48-volt mild hybrid technology starting on the A8 before rolling into the A6, A7, and Q8 - while petrol and diesel engines were further refined for Euro 6d compliance across all core models. Key Changes 48V mild hybrid (MHEV) system debuts on 2019 A8 (CREC 3.0 TFSI) and rolls into A6, A7, Q8 by 2018–2019 A4 B9 full model lineup consolidated with EA888 Gen 3 and EA288 engines - widest common-rail diesel range in the brand's history 2.0 TDI Euro 6d DFCA/DETA updates improve NOx performance following post-Dieselgate scrutiny PHEV variants expand: A3 e-tron, Q7 e-tron, and A8 PHEV join UK line-up RS3 (8V facelift) 2.5 TFSI DAZA replaces CEPA as benchmark five-cylinder performance engine Main Engines DJHA / DKRA / DNUA - 2.0 TFSI EA888 Gen 3 petrol (Audi A4, A5, Q5) DFCA / DETA / DERA - 2.0 TDI EA288 Euro 6d diesel (Audi A4, A5, A6, Q5) CREC / CGWB - 3.0 TFSI V6 petrol 48V MHEV (Audi A6, A7, A8, Q8) CRUA / CUPA - 3.0 TDI V6 diesel 48V MHEV (Audi A6, A7, A8, Q7) DAZA - 2.5 TFSI five-cylinder petrol (Audi RS3, TT RS) Popular Models Audi A4 (B9) Audi A6 (C8) Audi A7 (4K) Audi Q5 (80A) Audi Q8 (4M) Audi RS3 (8V facelift) Known For MHEV 48V system on A6/A8 is praised for smooth coasting and marginal fuel efficiency gains, but mild hybrid battery and belt-starter-generator failures carry significant replacement cost at 80,000+ miles EA888 Gen 3 in B9 A4 remains robust - water pump failures and thermostat housing leaks are the most common workshop complaints in this generation 2.5 TFSI DAZA in RS3 is widely regarded as one of the finest performance five-cylinders on sale - reliable at standard power levels with a devoted aftermarket Euro 6d compliance on updated TDIs adds complexity to exhaust aftertreatment - SCR system urea injector failures are an emerging cost for high-mileage fleet buyers [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: DJHA, DKRA, DNUA, DFCA, DETA, DERA, CREC, CGWB, CRUA, CUPA, DAZA, DKZC, DLBA

years.preview: EA888 Gen 4 MHEV, PHEV Roll-Out & RS Performance Peak 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The EA888 fourth-generation petrol arrived with integrated 12V mild hybrid assistance as standard across the A3, A4, and Q3, completing Audi's full electrification of the combustion range - while RS models reached a performance pinnacle before wider electrification. Key Changes EA888 Gen 4 (DKZA, DLBA, DNTA) introduced in A3 (8Y) and updated A4 - features integrated mild hybrid belt system as standard RS6/RS7 C8 launched with 4.0 V8 biturbo MHEV 48V (DGUB) - 600ps flagship engine with mild hybrid assist New A3 (8Y) drops diesel option entirely - petrol-only for first time in UK A3 history Q5 and Q7 TFSI e PHEV models expand - 2.0 TFSI petrol paired with plug-in electric motor across SUV line-up RS e-tron GT (SSP platform) marks Audi's first fully electric RS model in the UK Main Engines DKZA / DLBA / DNTA - 2.0 TFSI EA888 Gen 4 petrol 12V MHEV (Audi A3, A4) DGUB - 4.0 V8 biturbo TFSI 48V MHEV petrol (Audi RS6, RS7) DKZC / DKZD - 1.5 TFSI petrol (Audi A3, Q3) DETA / DERA - 2.0 TDI diesel (Audi A4, A6, Q5, Q7) DGEA / DDSA - 2.0 TFSI PHEV petrol + electric (Audi Q5 TFSI e, A6 TFSI e) Popular Models Audi A3 (8Y) Audi A4 (B9 facelift) Audi Q5 (80A facelift) Audi RS6 (C8) Audi RS7 (C8) Audi Q3 (F3) Known For RS6 / RS7 4.0 V8 DGUB is a high-technology benchmark unit - reliable at standard output but very high rebuild cost if oil starvation issues occur on neglected service EA888 Gen 4 integrates mild hybrid from the factory, making a like-for-like engine swap more complex than previous generations - replacement specialists advise careful code matching New A3 (8Y) 1.5 TFSI in petrol-only form marks a generational shift in buyer profile - diesel demand for A3 effectively ends in the UK from 2020 PHEV battery degradation is an emerging concern for early TFSI e SUV owners - replacement engine demand is concentrated on the petrol element when the hybrid control module fails [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: DKZA, DLBA, DNTA, DKZC, DKZD, DGUB, DETA, DERA, DGEA, DDSA, DLYA
years.cta: Find 2020–2022 Audi replacement engines - V8 MHEV, EA888 Gen 4, and TDI units

years.preview: Euro 6e, Q6 e-tron & Final Combustion Generation 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Audi's current engine period represents the last full-range combustion generation before the planned ICE phase-out - with Euro 6e compliance updates, the continued 48V MHEV integration across TDI and TFSI, and the e-tron range expanding as the brand moves toward an electric-first future. Key Changes Euro 6e compliance updates across all remaining TDI and TFSI models - SCR and EGR system revisions New Q6 e-tron (2024) and A6 e-tron (2024) launched as fully electric platforms - PPE architecture RS3 (8Y) and TT RS continue with revised 2.5 TFSI DAZA as final five-cylinder iteration 48V MHEV now standard across all petrol A4, A5, A6, Q5, and Q7 combustion variants Combustion A4 and A5 (B9) enter end-of-production cycle - replaced by new generation A5 and A6 body styles Main Engines DKZA / DLBA - 2.0 TFSI EA888 Gen 4 48V MHEV (Audi A4, A5 final build) DETA / DCYA - 2.0 TDI 48V MHEV diesel (Audi Q5, Q7, A6) DAZA - 2.5 TFSI five-cylinder petrol (Audi RS3 8Y, TT RS final) DGUB - 4.0 V8 biturbo TFSI MHEV (Audi RS6, RS7, S8) N/A (BEV) - PPE electric platform (Audi Q6 e-tron, A6 e-tron) Popular Models Audi RS3 (8Y) Audi Q5 (80A) Audi A6 (C8 facelift) Audi Q7 (4M facelift) Audi RS6 (C8) Known For Final RS3 DAZA five-cylinder remains one of the most emotionally involving small-car engines in the UK market - strong residual values keep replacement demand elevated Euro 6e diesel updates increase sensor and control module complexity - specialist diagnosis is increasingly essential before committing to engine replacement vs repair EV transition pressure is visibly reducing new TDI registrations, creating a growing secondary market for quality reconditioned combustion units BEV e-tron platforms are outside traditional engine replacement scope - demand concentrated on remaining combustion A4, A6, Q5, and RS-model owners [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: DKZA, DLBA, DETA, DCYA, DAZA, DGUB, DLYA, DNTA

faq.h2: Audi Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Audi owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Audi engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt Audi engine typically costs £2,000–£6,000 depending on displacement and complexity, with entry-level 1.6/2.0 TDI units at the lower end and performance V6/V8 TFSI units at the higher end. Reconditioned units range £1,800–£5,200, while used engines start around £800–£3,500. Supply-and-fit labour adds £600–£1,500 depending on model accessibility. Main dealers often charge 40–60% more for equivalent work. *Compare Audi engine replacement prices now to see live quotes from UK specialists.*

items.question: What affects the cost of a Audi engine replacement?
items.answer: Four key factors drive pricing: engine size (2.0L vs 3.0/4.0L), condition (used vs reconditioned vs rebuilt), vehicle complexity (Quattro/AWD adds labour time), and service type (supply-only vs supply-and-fit). London and South East locations typically add 20–30% to labour costs. Engine availability for specific codes like CAGA or CASA also influences price volatility. Our comparison tool provides transparent, like-for-like quotes across all variables. *Get quotes from UK Audi engine specialists to see how your specific model affects pricing.*

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Audi?
items.answer: In most cases, yes—especially if the chassis, gearbox, and interior remain in good condition. A rebuilt Audi engine with upgraded components (e.g., revised piston rings for EA888, reinforced tensioners for 3.0 TDI) often outlasts the original unit. For an A4 or Q5 valued at £3,000–£8,000, a £2,500–£4,000 rebuilt engine preserves asset value far more cost-effectively than purchasing a replacement vehicle. *Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Audi engine makes financial sense for your model.*

items.question: What's included in a Audi engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply-and-fit service covers sourcing the correct engine code, removing the failed unit, installing the replacement with new gaskets/seals, refilling fluids and filters, conducting a post-fit diagnostic reset, and providing a workmanship warranty alongside the engine warranty. Disposal of the old engine and a post-installation test drive are typically included. Fitting costs range £600–£1,500 depending on model complexity and location. *Compare Audi engine supply and fit deals to find specialists offering comprehensive, warrantied installations.*

items.question: How long does a Audi engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common models like the A3 or A4 with widely available engine codes (e.g., CFFB, CAEB), expect 3–5 working days from engine arrival to completion. More complex applications—Q7 3.0 TDI, A8 V8, or Quattro variants—may require 5–7 days due to additional drivetrain disassembly. The biggest variable is engine availability; our network prioritises commonly replaced codes to minimise wait times. *Ask suppliers about current lead times for your specific Audi engine code when requesting quotes.*

items.answer: Yes—every reconditioned or rebuilt Audi engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Extended 24‑month coverage is often available on premium rebuilt and remanufactured units. Warranty typically covers major internal components: crankshaft, pistons, connecting rods, timing chain/belt assembly, and oil pump. Always confirm exact terms, exclusions, and labour coverage directly with your chosen supplier before proceeding. *Find reconditioned Audi engines with comprehensive warranty cover by comparing specialist quotes today.*

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Audi engine?
items.answer: Used Audi engines offer lower upfront cost (£800–£3,500) and suit temporary fixes or vehicles nearing end-of-life. Reconditioned units (£1,800–£5,200) include new seals, bearings, and upgraded components addressing known failure points—plus warranty protection and expected longevity of 100k+ miles. For failure-prone families like EA888 TFSI or EA189 TDI, a reconditioned engine with design revisions is strongly recommended for long-term ownership. *Compare used vs reconditioned Audi engine prices to weigh upfront savings against long-term reliability.*

items.question: What are the most common Audi engine failures?
items.answer: The most catastrophic, replacement-driving failures include: EA888 2.0 TFSI piston ring wear causing excessive oil consumption (A3/A4/Q5 2008–2016); EA189 2.0 TDI EGR cooler cracking and DPF blockage (A3/A4/Q5 2009–2017); 3.0 TDI V6 rear timing chain tensioner failure (A6/Q7 2010–2018); and EA211 1.4 TFSI timing chain stretch (A1/A3/Q2 2012–2018). These issues rarely permit economical repair once advanced, making replacement the most common outcome. *Compare rebuilt Audi engines with upgraded components designed to address these known failure points.*

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Audi engine?
items.answer: Always compare multiple quotes—prices for the same engine code can vary by £1,000+ across suppliers. Consider used engines for tight budgets or short-term ownership, but opt for reconditioned units with failure-point upgrades if you plan to keep the Audi long-term. Ask whether quotes include fitting, delivery, VAT, and warranty labour coverage to avoid hidden costs. Supply-only options can save money if you have a trusted independent garage. *Use our free comparison tool to get tailored Audi engine quotes with full cost transparency.*
